theme: The Office Remembers — History, Presence, and the Passage of Time

ideas.title: Worker Progress Ring on Office Avatars
ideas.description: Working characters on the office floor show no progress info — you have to switch to Dashboard to see how far along they are. Draw a thin SVG progress ring around each working character's avatar circle. In Office.jsx, for `char--working` characters, wrap `.char__avatar` in a `<svg>` overlay with a `<circle>` using `stroke-dasharray` and `stroke-dashoffset` to show completion percentage. Use the worker's `--role-color` for the ring stroke. The ring fills clockwise as progress goes 0→100%. At 100%, pulse the ring green once (`@keyframes ring-complete { 0% { stroke-width: 2 } 50% { stroke-width: 5; stroke: #22c55e } 100% { stroke-width: 2 } }`). WHY: The office floor is the flagship view but it's progress-blind. Users watch characters bob at desks with zero sense of momentum. A progress ring makes the floor self-sufficient — you can tell at a glance who's almost done, who just started, and who's stuck without ever leaving the tab.

ideas.title: Last Action Timestamp on Ghost Characters
ideas.description: Ghost characters (roster members not currently working) sit at their desks with zero context. The tooltip shows their role but not WHEN they last did something. Add a `ghost-last-seen` element inside the ghost OOO bubble: look up the worker's most recent activity entry from the activity log data and display 'Last seen: 2h ago' under their role. In Office.jsx, pass the `activity` array as a prop and find `activity.find(a => a.worker === name)?.timestamp`. Format with the existing `getRelativeTime()` utility. WHY: Ghosts without timestamps feel like decorations. With a 'last seen' label, each ghost tells a micro-story: 'Maya was here 20 minutes ago' vs 'Dave hasn't been around for 6 hours'. It makes the office feel like a place with memory, not just a snapshot.

ideas.title: Window Sky Reflects Actual Time of Day
ideas.description: The office window (`.office-window`) currently shows a static sky gradient hardcoded per vibe. Instead, compute sky color from the visitor's local clock: 6-8AM golden sunrise (`linear-gradient(180deg, #fcd34d, #fdba74)`), 8AM-5PM blue sky (`#60a5fa, #3b82f6`) with drifting CSS clouds, 5-7PM orange sunset (`#f97316, #7c2d12`), 7PM-6AM night sky (`#0f172a, #1e1b4b`) with twinkling stars. In Office.jsx, compute `const hour = new Date().getHours()` and select a sky config object `{ gradient, showStars, showClouds }`. Update every 60s via `useEffect` interval. The existing `.office-window__star` elements already have twinkle animations — just toggle their `display` based on time. Add 2-3 `.window-cloud` divs with `@keyframes cloud-drift { from { left: -20% } to { left: 110% } }` for daytime. WHY: The window is the only element that connects the virtual office to the real world. A sky that matches your actual time makes the office feel inhabited RIGHT NOW, not frozen in a screenshot. It's the kind of detail that makes someone on a different continent say 'wait, it's daytime there?'

ideas.title: Confetti Burst Uses Worker's Role Color
ideas.description: The confetti celebration when a worker completes a task uses hardcoded accent/green/yellow colors for all workers. Personalize it: in App.jsx's confetti trigger logic, pass the completing worker's `WORKER_ROLE_COLORS[name]` as the primary confetti color. In the confetti CSS, use `--confetti-color` custom property: `.confetti-piece:nth-child(3n) { background: var(--confetti-color, var(--accent)) }`. Set the property via inline style on `.confetti-layer`: `style={{ '--confetti-color': WORKER_ROLE_COLORS[workerName] }}`. Add a second shade by mixing the role color at 60% opacity for the alternating pieces. WHY: When Maya ships, the celebration should feel like HER celebration — purple confetti, not generic rainbow. It reinforces each worker's visual identity and makes completions feel personal. Over time, users will associate colors with workers unconsciously.

ideas.title: Idle Characters Interact with Each Other
ideas.description: Idle characters wander the lower floor area on fixed paths (`wander-1` through `wander-4`) that never intersect meaningfully. When two idle characters' wander paths bring them within 8% horizontal distance of each other (check via computed keyframe positions), show a tiny speech bubble between them with a random chat emoji (💬, 😂, 🤔, ☕, 👋). In Office.jsx, create a `useIdleProximity(idleWorkers)` hook that checks positions every 2s and returns pairs that are close. Render a `<div className='idle-chat-bubble'>` absolutely positioned between the two characters. The bubble fades in for 3s then fades out. WHY: Idle characters currently feel like they're in solitary confinement — wandering alone in silence. Even a tiny emoji exchange makes the office feel social. It's the difference between 'those are idle animations' and 'oh, Maya and Carlos are chatting by the coffee machine'.

ideas.title: Mini Activity Ticker on Office Floor
ideas.description: The office floor has zero awareness of the activity log. Add a slim ticker strip along the bottom edge of `.office-floor` (above the door), showing the latest 1-2 activity entries scrolling horizontally. In Office.jsx, render `<div className='office-ticker'>` with `position: absolute; bottom: 8%; left: 5%; right: 5%; height: 14px; overflow: hidden;`. Inside, a `<span className='office-ticker__text'>` with `@keyframes ticker-scroll { from { transform: translateX(100%) } to { transform: translateX(-100%) } }` over 20s. Content: `'{emoji} {worker} {message} • {time}'` from the latest 2 activity entries. WHY: Users who prefer the office view over the dashboard currently miss all activity events. A ticker bridges that gap without adding clutter — it's ambient information, like a news ticker in a real office lobby. It also creates motion in the lower dead zone of the floor.

ideas.title: Office Has a Memory Wall — Shipped Feature Gallery
ideas.description: Add a small picture frame element on the office wall (left side, below the whiteboard) that cycles through 'shipped' milestones. Each completed task gets a tiny commemorative entry: `{ worker, task, date, emoji }`. Store in `/workspace/data/shipped.json` (Bloberto appends on each completion). The frame in Office.jsx shows one at a time, rotating every 10s with a fade transition. On hover, show the full list as a scrollable tooltip. CSS: `.memory-wall { position: absolute; left: 1%; top: 60%; width: 6%; height: 10%; background: var(--surface2); border: 2px solid #b8860b; border-radius: 2px; }` with a tiny gold frame effect. WHY: Every office has photos on the wall. This one has shipped features. It creates a sense of history and accomplishment — new visitors can see what the team has built over time. It also gamifies completions: workers aren't just finishing tasks, they're earning a spot on the wall.
